title: Addison's Disease
oneLiner: Primary adrenal insufficiency causing deficient cortisol and aldosterone; presents with hyperpigmentation, hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia – a life-threatening mimic of sepsis.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic Vignette:** A 35-year-old woman with unexplained weight loss, fatigue, and progressively darkening skin over months presents to ED with vomiting, abdominal pain, and confusion. BP 85/50 (lying), HR 110, hyperpigmented knuckles and buccal mucosa. Serum Na 124, K 6.2, glucose 3.0. She is on no medications. She looks "septic" but has no fever or clear source.

- **Dissect the Vignette:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Darkening skin (knuckles, buccal) | Elevated ACTH from loss of cortisol feedback → melanocyte stimulation |
| Hypotension + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia | Aldosterone deficiency → salt wasting, volume depletion, K+ retention |
| Hypoglycemia | Cortisol deficiency → impaired gluconeogenesis, increased insulin sensitivity |
| Confusion / coma | Cerebral edema from hyponatremia + hypoglycemia |
| No fever / infection source | Not true sepsis – adrenal crisis can mimic septic shock |

- **Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

1. **"Late-onset congenital adrenal hyperplasia"** in a younger patient – ambiguous genitalia at birth may have been missed, then presents with Addisonian crisis under stress.
2. **"Isolated hyperkalemia"** – some patients present with asymptomatic hyperkalemia on routine labs, especially if volume status is maintained by salt craving.
3. **"Chronic fatigue + vitiligo"** – remember autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes (APS1/2): Addison's + vitiligo + hypothyroidism + type 1 diabetes. The patient may have prior autoimmune diagnoses but no adrenal crisis yet.
4. **"Post-partum collapse"** – Sheehan's syndrome (pituitary apoplexy) causes secondary adrenal insufficiency, but can mimic primary Addison's if ACTH is low. Crucially, no hyperpigmentation.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain-language definition:** Addison’s disease is the failure of the adrenal cortex to produce sufficient **cortisol** and **aldosterone** – the hormones that keep your blood pressure up, your blood sugar stable, and your electrolyte balance normal. Without them, the body cannot mount a stress response.

- **Why it matters:** Though rare (≈100–140 per million), it is a **"can't-miss diagnosis"** because an adrenal crisis is rapidly fatal if untreated. It is commonly misdiagnosed as sepsis, gastroenteritis, or eating disorder. Junior doctors often miss it because they don't check cortisol in the "septic" patient who doesn't respond to fluids. The stakes: death from shock, hypoglycemia, or cardiac arrhythmia within hours if not treated. The pitfall: giving high-dose benzodiazepines or insulin without correcting cortisol first → prolonged coma/uncontrolled hypoglycemia.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **The Story:** The adrenal cortex has three layers: zona glomerulosa (aldosterone), zona fasciculata (cortisol), zona reticularis (androgens). In primary adrenal insufficiency, all three are destroyed – usually by autoimmune attack (90% of cases). This removes the negative feedback on the pituitary, so **ACTH** and **MSH** (cleaved from same pro-opiomelanocortin precursor) skyrocket. MSH stimulates melanocytes → hyperpigmentation. Without cortisol → impaired gluconeogenesis → hypoglycemia; lack of permissive effect on catecholamines → hypotension, poor response to stress. Without aldosterone → renal loss of Na+ and water, retention of K+ → hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, volume depletion.

- **Mechanism → Sign:**

| Pathophys Mechanism | Clinical Sign |
|--------------------|---------------|
| ↓ Cortisol → loss of feedback → ↑ ACTH/MSH | Hyperpigmentation (sun-exposed areas, palmar creases, buccal mucosa, recent scars) |
| ↓ Aldosterone → Na+ wasting, water loss, K+ retention | Hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, volume depletion (postural hypotension, low JVP) |
| ↓ Cortisol → impaired gluconeogenesis + increased insulin sensitivity | Hypoglycemia (often fasting, can be severe) |
| ↓ Cortisol → inadequate vasopressor response to shock | Refractory hypotension (even to fluids) |
| ↓ Androgens (DHEA) | Loss of axillary/pubic hair (women especially), low libido |

- **Connection to Pattern Recognition:** The vignette's hyperpigmentation directly tells you it's **primary** (not secondary). The triad of hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypoglycemia should trigger immediate thought of adrenal crisis – not just “sepsis”. The absence of fever suggests non-infectious shock. The lack of response to IV fluids should prompt a bedside cortisol check.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Autoimmune** (most common, 70–90%) | Isolated or part of APS1/2 (polyendocrine) | Young–middle-aged female, other autoimmunity (vitiligo, hypothyroidism, T1DM) |
| **Infectious** | Tuberculosis (XDR, especially in endemic regions) | History of TB, Pott’s disease, apical lung changes on CXR |
| | HIV, CMV, fungal (histoplasmosis, cryptococcus) | Immunocompromised host, opportunistic infections |
| **Hemorrhagic / Infarction** | Adrenal haemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome) | Meningococcemia, anticoagulation, trauma, sepsis, DIC |
| | Bilateral adrenal infarction (rare) | Severe hypotension, anti-phospholipid syndrome |
| **Infiltrative** | Amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, hemochromatosis | Multi-organ involvement |
| **Metastatic** | Lung, breast, melanoma, lymphoma | Known malignancy, bilateral adrenal masses on CT |
| **Drug-induced** | Ketoconazole, etomidate (even single dose!), rifampicin, phenytoin | History of antifungal, anaesthetic (etomidate), anti-TB therapy |
| **Iatrogenic** | Bilateral adrenalectomy | Surgery for Cushing's or pheochromocytoma |
| **Genetic** | Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency) | Ambiguous genitalia, salt-wasting crisis in infancy |

- **Most common cause overall:** Autoimmune destruction – isolated or in polyendocrine syndromes. Sporadic, insidious onset.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
- **Diagnostic Criteria:** The gold standard is the **Synacthen (cosyntropin) test** – also called short ACTH stimulation test. Key threshold: **1-hour post-250mcg IV/IM ACTH cortisol < 500 nmol/L** is diagnostic of adrenal insufficiency. A baseline cortisol < 100 nmol/L is strongly suggestive.

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How To Interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| 8 am serum cortisol | Baseline adrenal function | First screen (only reliable if patient is clinically stable and not in crisis) | < 100 nmol/L → likely adrenal insufficiency; 100-500 → needs stimulation test; > 500 rules out |
| Short Synacthen test | Dynamic adrenal reserve | Confirms diagnosis; gives ACTH to see if adrenal can respond | Normal: cortisol rise > 500 at 30 or 60 min. Failure to rise → primary adrenal insufficiency |
| Plasma ACTH | Distinguish primary from secondary | Ordered with cortisol during Synacthen test | High ACTH (> 2x normal) → primary; low or inappropriately normal → secondary |
| Electrolytes + glucose | Emergent assessment | Guide immediate fluid/glucose therapy | Hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, hypoglycemia |
| Renin / aldosterone | Aldosterone status | Confirm primary vs secondary; guide fludrocortisone dose | High renin, low aldosterone → primary; low renin, low aldosterone → secondary |
| Adrenal antibodies (21-OH) | Autoimmune cause | If cause uncertain, helps guide screening for APS | Positive → autoimmune Addison's |
| Imaging (CT adrenals) | Structural cause | If TB, haemorrhage, metastases, or adrenal mass suspected | Small glands → autoimmune; large glands with calcification → TB; large masses → metastases |

- **Pattern-recognition:** The **single finding that confirms primary Addison's** is a high ACTH (> 150 pg/mL) with low cortisol (< 100). The **single finding that rules it out** is a normal Synacthen test (cortisol rise > 500). *If you suspect adrenal crisis, do not delay treatment for a Synacthen test – start hydrocortisone immediately, then test later.*
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
- **Immediate (First Hour)** – "Adrenal Crisis Protocol"
    - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl (Normal Saline) 500 mL stat IV over 15 mins, then run 1 L over 1 hour. Do NOT use dextrose-only fluids first – you must correct volume.
    - **Steroids:** **Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat** (NOT dexamethasone – it does not cross-react in cortisol assay and takes longer to work). Then 50 mg IV 6-hourly.
    - **Hypoglycemia:** If glucose < 3.0, give **50% dextrose 50 mL IV** (25g) after saline started. Do not give just dextrose without steroids.
    - **Stop!** Do NOT delay fluids/hydrocortisone for lab results. Do NOT give dexamethasone if you plan to do a Synacthen test later – it will not interfere with cortisol assay, but hydrocortisone will. However, in crisis, hydrocortisone is life-saving – test after.

- **Ward (24–72h)**
    - **Continue IV hydrocortisone** 50 mg IV q6h for 24h, then taper to oral as patient stabilises.
    - **Correct electrolytes:** monitor Na, K , glucose q6h. Usually fluids + steroids correct hyponatremia and hyperkalemia within 12–24h.
    - **Identify and treat trigger:** Look for infection (cultures, CXR, urinalysis). If sepsis, add IV antibiotics.
    - **Start fludrocortisone** only after hydrocortisone taper begins (please note: fludrocortisone is mineralocorticoid; hydrocortisone at high doses also has mineralocorticoid effect). Usually start **Fludrocortisone 0.1 mg PO daily** once oral intake resumes.

- **Long-Term**
    - **Hydrocortisone (oral):** 15–25 mg total daily dose (e.g., 10 mg on waking, 5 mg lunch, 5 mg evening) – mimic circadian rhythm. Adjust for stress (see below).
    - **Fludrocortisone (oral):** 0.05–0.2 mg daily. Dose titrated to supine-to-standing BP, electrolytes, and renin.
    - **Stress dosing:** For minor illness (fever, flu, tooth extraction) – **double hydrocortisone dose** for duration of stress. For major stress (surgery, trauma, severe infection) – IV hydrocortisone 100 mg q6h until recovery.
    - **Monitoring:** Annual 8 am cortisol, ACTH, renin, electrolytes; DEXA scan for bone density after >5 years of glucocorticoid therapy.
    - **Education:** Patient must carry **steroid card**, **MedicAlert bracelet**, and have an **emergency hydrocortisone injection kit** (100 mg IM). Teach family how to inject.

- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**
    - ❌ Do **NOT** give dexamethasone instead of hydrocortisone if you suspect adrenal crisis (delayed onset of action, no mineralocorticoid activity at low dose).
    - ❌ Do **NOT** give 5% dextrose as initial fluid – it dilutes Na further and provides no volume support.
    - ❌ Do **NOT** wait for Synacthen test results in suspected crisis – start treatment.
    - ❌ Do **NOT** forget steroids during stress – even a missed dose in a stable patient can precipitate crisis.
    - ❌ Do **NOT** use fludrocortisone in secondary adrenal insufficiency (renin–aldosterone axis intact).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Primary Addison's | Septic Shock | Hypovolemic Shock (e.g., GI bleed) | Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency |
|---------|-------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Abdominal pain (vague) | Often localised to infection source | Severe pain if bleeding, else none | None |
| **Exam** | Hyperpigmentation (skin + buccal), loss of pubic/axillary hair (women), postural hypotension | Fever, tachycardia, warm or cold shock, source of infection | Pale, clammy, cool, flat neck veins, psoas sign if retroperitoneal bleed | Pallor, no hyperpigmentation, sparse axillary/pubic hair, may have mass (pituitary tumour) |
| **ECG** | Hyperkalemia changes: peaked T waves, wide QRS, sine wave (if K >7.0) | May have atrial fibrillation/tachycardia, but no specific hyperkalemia pattern | Tachycardia, low voltage (if large bleed) | Usually normal |
| **Labs** | Na ↓, K ↑, glu ↓, cortisol ↓, ACTH ↑, renin ↑, aldosterone ↓, metabolic acidosis | WBC ↑, lactate ↑, procalcitonin ↑, cortisol may be low (but ACTH normal), K often normal (may be low if vomiting) | Hb ↓, lactate ↑, K normal, electrolytes variable, cortisol normal or high, ACTH normal | Na ↓, glu ↓, K normal or low, ACTH ↓, renin normal, aldosterone normal/low |
| **Key differentiator** | Hyperpigmentation + high ACTH + hyperkalemia | Fever + source + high procalcitonin + normal ACTH | History of bleeding + drop in Hb + no hyperpigmentation | No hyperpigmentation + low ACTH + normal K |

- **Additional mimics:** Salt-losing congenital adrenal hyperplasia (infants), panhypopituitarism, primary hyperaldosteronism (opposite electrolytes: hypokalemia, high BP). Use ACTH, renin, and aldosterone to differentiate.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Adrenal Crisis (acute decompensation)**
    - How it presents: Profound hypotension (systolic <80), vomiting, confusion, hypoglycaemia, hyperkalemia arrhythmias. Usually triggered by illness, surgery, or forgotten steroids.
    - Key signs: Refractory to fluids ± vasopressors; hyperpigmentation clues; ECG of hyperkalemia.
    - What to do: IV NS 1L stat, IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, IV dextrose 50% if hypoglycaemia. Call ICU.
- **Hyperkalemia-Induced Cardiac Arrest**
    - How it presents: Syncope, arrhythmia (bradycardia, ventricular tachycardia, asystole). ECG: peaked T waves → widened QRS → sine wave.
    - What to do: Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV over 2–5 min to stabilise myocardium, then insulin + dextrose, then correction of acidosis, and ultimately steroids to fix the cause.
- **Severe Hyponatremia (<120)**
    - How it presents: Confusion, seizure, brainstem herniation.
    - What to do: Give 0.9% NS – not hypertonic saline (unless seizure/coma). The hyponatremia is primarily volume depletion, not SIADH. Rapid correction can occur with fluids + steroids.
- **Chronic Malnutrition and Muscle Wasting**
    - How it presents: Progressive weight loss, poor exercise tolerance.
    - Key signs: Low BMI, muscle atrophy, low bone density.
    - What to do: Optimise glucocorticoid replacement + ensure adequate caloric intake.
- **Late Presentation: “Comatose, shocked, scar-red”**
    - The stem: An elderly patient found collapsed at home. Hypoglycaemia 1.8, Na 118, K 7.0, no fever. Hyperpigmentation may be subtle from chronic sun exposure. The clue: **old scars are hyperpigmented** (because ACTH stimulates melanocytes even in scar tissue). Look for pigmented scars, palmar creases, buccal mucosa.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonic: ADDISON'S** – recall key features:
    - **A** – Adrenal failure (most common cause: autoimmune)
    - **D** – Dark skin (hyperpigmentation)
    - **D** – Deficient cortisol, aldosterone
    - **I** – Insensible to stress (hypotension, hypoglycaemia)
    - **S** – Salt wasting (hyponatremia)
    - **O** – Overwhelming K+ (hyperkalemia)
    - **N** – Nightmare if missed (adrenal crisis)
    - **S** – Steroid therapy for life
- **Mnemonic: PACK** for causes of primary adrenal insufficiency:
    - **P** – Polyendocrine (autoimmune)
    - **A** – Adrenal haemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen)
    - **C** – CAH (congenital adrenal hyperplasia)
    - **K** – Killers: TB, fungal, HIV, metastases
- **Pearls (Pattern Recognition Cues):**

    1. **"If they are septic but do not respond to fluids, check a cortisol level."** This is the single most important pearl.
    2. **Hyperpigmentation always points to primary, not secondary.** If you see it, think Addison’s.
    3. **Hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + hypoglycemia is pathognomonic for adrenal crisis** – treat immediately.
    4. **ECG hyperkalemia pattern in a shocked patient with abdominal pain** → push steroids, not just calcium.
    5. **“Stress dosing” is not optional.** Fever > 38.5°C, vomiting, surgery, or significant trauma: double or triple oral hydrocortisone or give IV.
    6. **Do not stop steroids abruptly.** Taper if high dose >3 weeks to avoid secondary adrenal insufficiency.
    7. **Look for other autoimmune diseases** – screen for thyroid disease, type 1 DM, pernicious anemia, vitiligo. Addison’s is often part of APS2.

- **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
    - Misinterpreting low cortisol as “normal” because it is within the reference range (the patient is stressed – appropriate cortisol should be very high). Use dynamic test.
    - Giving dexamethasone as the only steroid for crisis (it has no mineralocorticoid effect and slow onset).
    - Starting fludrocortisone before volume status is corrected (can worsen hyperkalemia? Actually it helps, but better to start after acute phase).
    - Forgetting to give patient a steroid card and emergency kit at discharge.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2** | ≥ 5 | Immediate medical review, commence adrenal crisis protocol if suspected |
| **Systolic BP** | < 90 mmHg despite 1 L NS | Call ICU / Medical Registrar – may need vasopressors |
| **GCS** | < 15 (especially if no hypoglycaemia) | Urgent CT head for possible cerebral oedema; call anaesthetics/ICU |
| **Blood glucose** | < 3.0 with altered conscious level | Give IV 50% dextrose 50 mL + 100 mg hydrocortisone; call senior |
| **Serum K+** | > 6.5 mmol/L or ECG: peaked T + widened QRS | Treat hyperkalemia urgently (calcium, insulin/dextrose); call ICU |
| **Lactate** | > 4.0 mmol/L despite IV fluids | Consider critical illness / septic shock; escalate critical care |
| **Respiratory compromise** | RR > 25, SpO2 < 92% on O2 | ICU review – may need respiratory support if severe metabolic acidosis |
| **Failure to improve** | No improvement in BP or GCS after 1 h of treatment | Call medical registrar / ICU outreach – consider CT adrenal for haemorrhage |

- **When to call your senior:** Any suspicion of adrenal crisis (hypotension + hypoglycaemia + hyponatremia/hyperkalemia). Do not wait for lab confirmation – start steroids and call.
- **When to call ICU:** Refractory hypotension requiring vasopressors, GCS < 13, respiratory failure, hyperkalemia with ECG changes, needed multiple doses of dextrose for hypoglycaemia.

rawMarkdown:
## Addison's Disease

**One-liner:** Primary adrenal insufficiency causing deficient cortisol and aldosterone; presents with hyperpigmentation, hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia – a life-threatening mimic of sepsis.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

- **Classic Vignette:** A 35-year-old woman with unexplained weight loss, fatigue, and progressively darkening skin over months presents to ED with vomiting, abdominal pain, and confusion. BP 85/50 (lying), HR 110, hyperpigmented knuckles and buccal mucosa. Serum Na 124, K 6.2, glucose 3.0. She is on no medications. She looks "septic" but has no fever or clear source.

- **Dissect the Vignette:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Darkening skin (knuckles, buccal) | Elevated ACTH from loss of cortisol feedback → melanocyte stimulation |
| Hypotension + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia | Aldosterone deficiency → salt wasting, volume depletion, K+ retention |
| Hypoglycemia | Cortisol deficiency → impaired gluconeogenesis, increased insulin sensitivity |
| Confusion / coma | Cerebral edema from hyponatremia + hypoglycemia |
| No fever / infection source | Not true sepsis – adrenal crisis can mimic septic shock |

- **Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

1. **"Late-onset congenital adrenal hyperplasia"** in a younger patient – ambiguous genitalia at birth may have been missed, then presents with Addisonian crisis under stress.
2. **"Isolated hyperkalemia"** – some patients present with asymptomatic hyperkalemia on routine labs, especially if volume status is maintained by salt craving.
3. **"Chronic fatigue + vitiligo"** – remember autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes (APS1/2): Addison's + vitiligo + hypothyroidism + type 1 diabetes. The patient may have prior autoimmune diagnoses but no adrenal crisis yet.
4. **"Post-partum collapse"** – Sheehan's syndrome (pituitary apoplexy) causes secondary adrenal insufficiency, but can mimic primary Addison's if ACTH is low. Crucially, no hyperpigmentation.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

- **Plain-language definition:** Addison’s disease is the failure of the adrenal cortex to produce sufficient **cortisol** and **aldosterone** – the hormones that keep your blood pressure up, your blood sugar stable, and your electrolyte balance normal. Without them, the body cannot mount a stress response.

- **Why it matters:** Though rare (≈100–140 per million), it is a **"can't-miss diagnosis"** because an adrenal crisis is rapidly fatal if untreated. It is commonly misdiagnosed as sepsis, gastroenteritis, or eating disorder. Junior doctors often miss it because they don't check cortisol in the "septic" patient who doesn't respond to fluids. The stakes: death from shock, hypoglycemia, or cardiac arrhythmia within hours if not treated. The pitfall: giving high-dose benzodiazepines or insulin without correcting cortisol first → prolonged coma/uncontrolled hypoglycemia.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

- **The Story:** The adrenal cortex has three layers: zona glomerulosa (aldosterone), zona fasciculata (cortisol), zona reticularis (androgens). In primary adrenal insufficiency, all three are destroyed – usually by autoimmune attack (90% of cases). This removes the negative feedback on the pituitary, so **ACTH** and **MSH** (cleaved from same pro-opiomelanocortin precursor) skyrocket. MSH stimulates melanocytes → hyperpigmentation. Without cortisol → impaired gluconeogenesis → hypoglycemia; lack of permissive effect on catecholamines → hypotension, poor response to stress. Without aldosterone → renal loss of Na+ and water, retention of K+ → hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, volume depletion.

- **Mechanism → Sign:**

| Pathophys Mechanism | Clinical Sign |
|--------------------|---------------|
| ↓ Cortisol → loss of feedback → ↑ ACTH/MSH | Hyperpigmentation (sun-exposed areas, palmar creases, buccal mucosa, recent scars) |
| ↓ Aldosterone → Na+ wasting, water loss, K+ retention | Hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, volume depletion (postural hypotension, low JVP) |
| ↓ Cortisol → impaired gluconeogenesis + increased insulin sensitivity | Hypoglycemia (often fasting, can be severe) |
| ↓ Cortisol → inadequate vasopressor response to shock | Refractory hypotension (even to fluids) |
| ↓ Androgens (DHEA) | Loss of axillary/pubic hair (women especially), low libido |

- **Connection to Pattern Recognition:** The vignette's hyperpigmentation directly tells you it's **primary** (not secondary). The triad of hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypoglycemia should trigger immediate thought of adrenal crisis – not just “sepsis”. The absence of fever suggests non-infectious shock. The lack of response to IV fluids should prompt a bedside cortisol check.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Autoimmune** (most common, 70–90%) | Isolated or part of APS1/2 (polyendocrine) | Young–middle-aged female, other autoimmunity (vitiligo, hypothyroidism, T1DM) |
| **Infectious** | Tuberculosis (XDR, especially in endemic regions) | History of TB, Pott’s disease, apical lung changes on CXR |
| | HIV, CMV, fungal (histoplasmosis, cryptococcus) | Immunocompromised host, opportunistic infections |
| **Hemorrhagic / Infarction** | Adrenal haemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome) | Meningococcemia, anticoagulation, trauma, sepsis, DIC |
| | Bilateral adrenal infarction (rare) | Severe hypotension, anti-phospholipid syndrome |
| **Infiltrative** | Amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, hemochromatosis | Multi-organ involvement |
| **Metastatic** | Lung, breast, melanoma, lymphoma | Known malignancy, bilateral adrenal masses on CT |
| **Drug-induced** | Ketoconazole, etomidate (even single dose!), rifampicin, phenytoin | History of antifungal, anaesthetic (etomidate), anti-TB therapy |
| **Iatrogenic** | Bilateral adrenalectomy | Surgery for Cushing's or pheochromocytoma |
| **Genetic** | Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency) | Ambiguous genitalia, salt-wasting crisis in infancy |

- **Most common cause overall:** Autoimmune destruction – isolated or in polyendocrine syndromes. Sporadic, insidious onset.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

- **Diagnostic Criteria:** The gold standard is the **Synacthen (cosyntropin) test** – also called short ACTH stimulation test. Key threshold: **1-hour post-250mcg IV/IM ACTH cortisol < 500 nmol/L** is diagnostic of adrenal insufficiency. A baseline cortisol < 100 nmol/L is strongly suggestive.

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How To Interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| 8 am serum cortisol | Baseline adrenal function | First screen (only reliable if patient is clinically stable and not in crisis) | < 100 nmol/L → likely adrenal insufficiency; 100-500 → needs stimulation test; > 500 rules out |
| Short Synacthen test | Dynamic adrenal reserve | Confirms diagnosis; gives ACTH to see if adrenal can respond | Normal: cortisol rise > 500 at 30 or 60 min. Failure to rise → primary adrenal insufficiency |
| Plasma ACTH | Distinguish primary from secondary | Ordered with cortisol during Synacthen test | High ACTH (> 2x normal) → primary; low or inappropriately normal → secondary |
| Electrolytes + glucose | Emergent assessment | Guide immediate fluid/glucose therapy | Hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, hypoglycemia |
| Renin / aldosterone | Aldosterone status | Confirm primary vs secondary; guide fludrocortisone dose | High renin, low aldosterone → primary; low renin, low aldosterone → secondary |
| Adrenal antibodies (21-OH) | Autoimmune cause | If cause uncertain, helps guide screening for APS | Positive → autoimmune Addison's |
| Imaging (CT adrenals) | Structural cause | If TB, haemorrhage, metastases, or adrenal mass suspected | Small glands → autoimmune; large glands with calcification → TB; large masses → metastases |

- **Pattern-recognition:** The **single finding that confirms primary Addison's** is a high ACTH (> 150 pg/mL) with low cortisol (< 100). The **single finding that rules it out** is a normal Synacthen test (cortisol rise > 500). *If you suspect adrenal crisis, do not delay treatment for a Synacthen test – start hydrocortisone immediately, then test later.*

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

- **Immediate (First Hour)** – "Adrenal Crisis Protocol"
    - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl (Normal Saline) 500 mL stat IV over 15 mins, then run 1 L over 1 hour. Do NOT use dextrose-only fluids first – you must correct volume.
    - **Steroids:** **Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat** (NOT dexamethasone – it does not cross-react in cortisol assay and takes longer to work). Then 50 mg IV 6-hourly.
    - **Hypoglycemia:** If glucose < 3.0, give **50% dextrose 50 mL IV** (25g) after saline started. Do not give just dextrose without steroids.
    - **Stop!** Do NOT delay fluids/hydrocortisone for lab results. Do NOT give dexamethasone if you plan to do a Synacthen test later – it will not interfere with cortisol assay, but hydrocortisone will. However, in crisis, hydrocortisone is life-saving – test after.

- **Ward (24–72h)**
    - **Continue IV hydrocortisone** 50 mg IV q6h for 24h, then taper to oral as patient stabilises.
    - **Correct electrolytes:** monitor Na, K , glucose q6h. Usually fluids + steroids correct hyponatremia and hyperkalemia within 12–24h.
    - **Identify and treat trigger:** Look for infection (cultures, CXR, urinalysis). If sepsis, add IV antibiotics.
    - **Start fludrocortisone** only after hydrocortisone taper begins (please note: fludrocortisone is mineralocorticoid; hydrocortisone at high doses also has mineralocorticoid effect). Usually start **Fludrocortisone 0.1 mg PO daily** once oral intake resumes.

- **Long-Term**
    - **Hydrocortisone (oral):** 15–25 mg total daily dose (e.g., 10 mg on waking, 5 mg lunch, 5 mg evening) – mimic circadian rhythm. Adjust for stress (see below).
    - **Fludrocortisone (oral):** 0.05–0.2 mg daily. Dose titrated to supine-to-standing BP, electrolytes, and renin.
    - **Stress dosing:** For minor illness (fever, flu, tooth extraction) – **double hydrocortisone dose** for duration of stress. For major stress (surgery, trauma, severe infection) – IV hydrocortisone 100 mg q6h until recovery.
    - **Monitoring:** Annual 8 am cortisol, ACTH, renin, electrolytes; DEXA scan for bone density after >5 years of glucocorticoid therapy.
    - **Education:** Patient must carry **steroid card**, **MedicAlert bracelet**, and have an **emergency hydrocortisone injection kit** (100 mg IM). Teach family how to inject.

- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**
    - ❌ Do **NOT** give dexamethasone instead of hydrocortisone if you suspect adrenal crisis (delayed onset of action, no mineralocorticoid activity at low dose).
    - ❌ Do **NOT** give 5% dextrose as initial fluid – it dilutes Na further and provides no volume support.
    - ❌ Do **NOT** wait for Synacthen test results in suspected crisis – start treatment.
    - ❌ Do **NOT** forget steroids during stress – even a missed dose in a stable patient can precipitate crisis.
    - ❌ Do **NOT** use fludrocortisone in secondary adrenal insufficiency (renin–aldosterone axis intact).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Primary Addison's | Septic Shock | Hypovolemic Shock (e.g., GI bleed) | Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency |
|---------|-------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Abdominal pain (vague) | Often localised to infection source | Severe pain if bleeding, else none | None |
| **Exam** | Hyperpigmentation (skin + buccal), loss of pubic/axillary hair (women), postural hypotension | Fever, tachycardia, warm or cold shock, source of infection | Pale, clammy, cool, flat neck veins, psoas sign if retroperitoneal bleed | Pallor, no hyperpigmentation, sparse axillary/pubic hair, may have mass (pituitary tumour) |
| **ECG** | Hyperkalemia changes: peaked T waves, wide QRS, sine wave (if K >7.0) | May have atrial fibrillation/tachycardia, but no specific hyperkalemia pattern | Tachycardia, low voltage (if large bleed) | Usually normal |
| **Labs** | Na ↓, K ↑, glu ↓, cortisol ↓, ACTH ↑, renin ↑, aldosterone ↓, metabolic acidosis | WBC ↑, lactate ↑, procalcitonin ↑, cortisol may be low (but ACTH normal), K often normal (may be low if vomiting) | Hb ↓, lactate ↑, K normal, electrolytes variable, cortisol normal or high, ACTH normal | Na ↓, glu ↓, K normal or low, ACTH ↓, renin normal, aldosterone normal/low |
| **Key differentiator** | Hyperpigmentation + high ACTH + hyperkalemia | Fever + source + high procalcitonin + normal ACTH | History of bleeding + drop in Hb + no hyperpigmentation | No hyperpigmentation + low ACTH + normal K |

- **Additional mimics:** Salt-losing congenital adrenal hyperplasia (infants), panhypopituitarism, primary hyperaldosteronism (opposite electrolytes: hypokalemia, high BP). Use ACTH, renin, and aldosterone to differentiate.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Adrenal Crisis (acute decompensation)**
    - How it presents: Profound hypotension (systolic <80), vomiting, confusion, hypoglycaemia, hyperkalemia arrhythmias. Usually triggered by illness, surgery, or forgotten steroids.
    - Key signs: Refractory to fluids ± vasopressors; hyperpigmentation clues; ECG of hyperkalemia.
    - What to do: IV NS 1L stat, IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, IV dextrose 50% if hypoglycaemia. Call ICU.
- **Hyperkalemia-Induced Cardiac Arrest**
    - How it presents: Syncope, arrhythmia (bradycardia, ventricular tachycardia, asystole). ECG: peaked T waves → widened QRS → sine wave.
    - What to do: Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV over 2–5 min to stabilise myocardium, then insulin + dextrose, then correction of acidosis, and ultimately steroids to fix the cause.
- **Severe Hyponatremia (<120)**
    - How it presents: Confusion, seizure, brainstem herniation.
    - What to do: Give 0.9% NS – not hypertonic saline (unless seizure/coma). The hyponatremia is primarily volume depletion, not SIADH. Rapid correction can occur with fluids + steroids.
- **Chronic Malnutrition and Muscle Wasting**
    - How it presents: Progressive weight loss, poor exercise tolerance.
    - Key signs: Low BMI, muscle atrophy, low bone density.
    - What to do: Optimise glucocorticoid replacement + ensure adequate caloric intake.
- **Late Presentation: “Comatose, shocked, scar-red”**
    - The stem: An elderly patient found collapsed at home. Hypoglycaemia 1.8, Na 118, K 7.0, no fever. Hyperpigmentation may be subtle from chronic sun exposure. The clue: **old scars are hyperpigmented** (because ACTH stimulates melanocytes even in scar tissue). Look for pigmented scars, palmar creases, buccal mucosa.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

- **Mnemonic: ADDISON'S** – recall key features:
    - **A** – Adrenal failure (most common cause: autoimmune)
    - **D** – Dark skin (hyperpigmentation)
    - **D** – Deficient cortisol, aldosterone
    - **I** – Insensible to stress (hypotension, hypoglycaemia)
    - **S** – Salt wasting (hyponatremia)
    - **O** – Overwhelming K+ (hyperkalemia)
    - **N** – Nightmare if missed (adrenal crisis)
    - **S** – Steroid therapy for life
- **Mnemonic: PACK** for causes of primary adrenal insufficiency:
    - **P** – Polyendocrine (autoimmune)
    - **A** – Adrenal haemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen)
    - **C** – CAH (congenital adrenal hyperplasia)
    - **K** – Killers: TB, fungal, HIV, metastases
- **Pearls (Pattern Recognition Cues):**

    1. **"If they are septic but do not respond to fluids, check a cortisol level."** This is the single most important pearl.
    2. **Hyperpigmentation always points to primary, not secondary.** If you see it, think Addison’s.
    3. **Hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + hypoglycemia is pathognomonic for adrenal crisis** – treat immediately.
    4. **ECG hyperkalemia pattern in a shocked patient with abdominal pain** → push steroids, not just calcium.
    5. **“Stress dosing” is not optional.** Fever > 38.5°C, vomiting, surgery, or significant trauma: double or triple oral hydrocortisone or give IV.
    6. **Do not stop steroids abruptly.** Taper if high dose >3 weeks to avoid secondary adrenal insufficiency.
    7. **Look for other autoimmune diseases** – screen for thyroid disease, type 1 DM, pernicious anemia, vitiligo. Addison’s is often part of APS2.

- **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
    - Misinterpreting low cortisol as “normal” because it is within the reference range (the patient is stressed – appropriate cortisol should be very high). Use dynamic test.
    - Giving dexamethasone as the only steroid for crisis (it has no mineralocorticoid effect and slow onset).
    - Starting fludrocortisone before volume status is corrected (can worsen hyperkalemia? Actually it helps, but better to start after acute phase).
    - Forgetting to give patient a steroid card and emergency kit at discharge.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2** | ≥ 5 | Immediate medical review, commence adrenal crisis protocol if suspected |
| **Systolic BP** | < 90 mmHg despite 1 L NS | Call ICU / Medical Registrar – may need vasopressors |
| **GCS** | < 15 (especially if no hypoglycaemia) | Urgent CT head for possible cerebral oedema; call anaesthetics/ICU |
| **Blood glucose** | < 3.0 with altered conscious level | Give IV 50% dextrose 50 mL + 100 mg hydrocortisone; call senior |
| **Serum K+** | > 6.5 mmol/L or ECG: peaked T + widened QRS | Treat hyperkalemia urgently (calcium, insulin/dextrose); call ICU |
| **Lactate** | > 4.0 mmol/L despite IV fluids | Consider critical illness / septic shock; escalate critical care |
| **Respiratory compromise** | RR > 25, SpO2 < 92% on O2 | ICU review – may need respiratory support if severe metabolic acidosis |
| **Failure to improve** | No improvement in BP or GCS after 1 h of treatment | Call medical registrar / ICU outreach – consider CT adrenal for haemorrhage |

- **When to call your senior:** Any suspicion of adrenal crisis (hypotension + hypoglycaemia + hyponatremia/hyperkalemia). Do not wait for lab confirmation – start steroids and call.
- **When to call ICU:** Refractory hypotension requiring vasopressors, GCS < 13, respiratory failure, hyperkalemia with ECG changes, needed multiple doses of dextrose for hypoglycaemia.
